content.problemStatement:
Chef has a binary string SSS of length NNN. 

Chef can perform the following operation on SSS any number of times


Insert any character (000 or 111) at any position in SSS.
Find the minimum number of operations Chef needs to perform so that no two consecutive characters are same in SSS.


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN — the length of the binary string SSS.
The second line of each test case contains a binary string SSS of length NNN containing 000s and 111s only.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the minimum number of operations Chef needs to perform so that no two consecutive characters are same in SSS.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 10^6
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N, the length of the binary string S; the second line contains the binary string S of length N consisting only of characters '0' and '1'.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer on its own line – the minimum number of insert operations required so that the resulting string has no two equal consecutive characters.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: "11" has one pair of equal neighbours, so 1 insertion is needed.\nTest case 2: "0101" already alternates, so 0 insertions.\nTest case 3: "00100" has equal pairs at positions (0,1) and (3,4), total 2 insertions.

solution.approach: The only way two equal characters can become non‑adjacent is to insert a different character between them. For a maximal block of equal characters of length L we need exactly L‑1 insertions (place the opposite character between each pair). Therefore the answer for a test case is simply the number of positions i (0‑based) such that S[i] == S[i+1]. This can be obtained by a single linear scan of the string.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N is the length of the string. Overall O(total length of all strings).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) additional space besides the input string.

code.Python3:
import sys

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it))
        S = next(it)
        ans = sum(1 for i in range(N-1) if S[i]==S[i+1])
        out_lines.append(str(ans))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
